planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie cordgrass is a dominant native of tallgrass prairie wetlands and edges and is one of the best tall grasses for consistently wet, even seasonally flooded conditions in the landscape.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie cordgrass spreads rhizomatously and can form large colonies in suitable wet conditions — allow ample space or use rhizome barriers to contain it. Spartina pectinata is a tall, imposing native grass of the tallgrass prairie region and eastern coastal plain, growing 4–7 feet tall with sharply-edged, arching leaves and distinctive comb-like (pectinate) flower spikes in late summer that are striking in silhouette against the sky; the leaves turn rich golden-yellow in fall and bleach to an attractive tan in winter. An extremely valuable grass for erosion control on wet slopes, rain gardens, bioswales, pond margins, and stream banks where its vigorous rhizome system stabilizes soil; it tolerates seasonal flooding better than almost any other native upland grass. The variegated cultivar 'Aureomarginata' has yellow-striped leaves.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in spring in dry to moist, rocky to sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas mulberry is the native mulberry of the dry Trans-Pecos region and Hill Country, extremely drought-tolerant and valuable for wildlife food in xeric landscapes.

directions: Plant container-grown trees or hardwood cuttings in spring in dry to moderately moist, rocky, sandy, or caliche soils in full sun to light shade; Texas mulberry once established is highly drought-tolerant and requires no supplemental irrigation in zone 7–10. Texas mulberry is a small native tree or large shrub (8–20 feet) of the dry Hill Country, Trans-Pecos, and Chihuahuan Desert of Texas and New Mexico, with smaller leaves and a more drought-tolerant constitution than any other native American mulberry; the sweet, dark purple to black mulberries ripen in late spring and are an extremely important food source for numerous bird species including cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, and orioles, as well as mammals from foxes to deer. Excellent as a water-conserving landscape tree for dry zone 7–10 gardens where conventional mulberries may struggle; the fruit is edible and excellent fresh or in pies and preserves.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring germination, or start indoors and transplant in early spring; wine cups develops a deep taproot that makes it extremely drought-tolerant once established but also means it should not be moved after planting — choose the site carefully.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall in sandy, gravelly, or well-drained soil in full sun; do not transplant established plants as the deep taproot is easily damaged. Callirhoe involucrata is one of the most beautiful and drought-tolerant native wildflowers of the central and southern prairies, producing sprawling stems 8–12 inches tall that spread 2–4 feet, covered from late spring through midsummer with brilliant magenta-crimson, cup-shaped flowers 1.5–2 inches across that are outstandingly vivid and seemingly fluorescent in full sunlight — the common name 'wine cups' perfectly captures the color and form. Absolutely drought-tolerant once the deep taproot establishes, and adapted to poor, rocky, and sandy soils; excellent for hot, dry slopes, rock gardens, and prairie plantings in zones 4–9. Native bees and bumblebees visit the flowers constantly.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, dry, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert globe mallow is native to Mojave and Sonoran Desert scrub and requires excellent drainage and minimal irrigation once established — it will rot quickly in wet or poorly drained soil.

directions: Sow scarified seeds directly in spring in extremely well-drained, dry, alkaline to neutral, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; germination is improved by lightly nicking the seed coat or soaking in hot water for 24 hours before planting. Desert globe mallow is a native wildflower of the Mojave and Sonoran Deserts producing distinctive cup-shaped, apricot-orange to deep coral-red flowers held in dense spikes above the grey-green, woolly, deeply lobed leaves from spring through fall in desert conditions — the flowers are among the most striking of any arid-region native and are a primary nectar source for the specialist globe mallow bee (Dieunomia). Once established it is extraordinarily drought-tolerant and long-lived; excellent for desert rock gardens, xeriscape plantings, and dry borders in zone 7–11 where summer heat and drought are extreme. The tiny hairs on the foliage can irritate skin and eyes — handle with gloves.
sources: Arizona State University Desert Plants Collection
Tucson Botanical Garden Native Plant Guide

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Jackson brier is the most ornamental of the southeastern Smilax species with glossy, lance-shaped leaves and is commonly used in Southern floral arrangements as greenery — it has been shipped as 'smilax' by florists for over a century.

directions: Plant in spring in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade against a trellis, fence, or tree for support; Smilax smallii climbs by tendrils and can reach 10–20 feet, providing dense evergreen coverage in shaded areas. Jackson brier is the most ornamental of the native southeastern Smilax species, producing long, lance-shaped, glossy dark green leaves on gracefully arching, somewhat thorny stems; it is the 'smilax' of traditional Southern floriculture — the garlands of small-leafed, shiny green vine used to decorate churches, ballrooms, and mantles throughout the South for weddings and holiday celebrations for well over 100 years. Small, greenish-white flowers in early spring are not showy but are fragrant, and small blue-black berries in fall are important food for mockingbirds, catbirds, and other wildlife. Valuable as a native evergreen shade vine in zone 7–10 landscapes.
